My name is Jane Addams. This is the story of how I opened Hull-House.

I had a dream. I wanted to live in a poor neighborhood. I wanted to help the people there. My friend Ellen Starr had the same dream.

In January, we came to Chicago. We looked for the right neighborhood. We walked through many streets. We talked to teachers, city workers, and newspaper reporters. We wanted to find a place where people needed help.

One day, we drove past an old house. It was large and beautiful. It had a wide porch with tall white pillars. I loved it right away.

I came back the next day to find it. I could not find it. I looked for many days. I could not find it anywhere. I gave up.

Three weeks later, a friend helped us choose a neighborhood. It was near three busy streets — Blue Island Avenue, Halsted Street, and Harrison Street. Many immigrants lived there. People had come from Italy, Germany, Russia, and many other countries. They lived close together but felt very alone.

On the very first day of our search, I saw the old house again. I was so happy. It was the house I had been looking for.

The house was old. It was called Hull-House, after the man who built it. We cleaned it. We fixed it. We opened the doors.

We opened Hull-House on September 18, 1889.

From the first day, neighbors came to us. Women brought their babies. Old men sat on the porch. Young people came to talk and to learn. We started art classes and reading groups. We helped mothers find work. We helped immigrants learn English.

Our neighbors were poor, but they were not hopeless. They wanted better lives for their children. They wanted to belong.

One cold evening, a woman knocked on our door. She was tired and alone. She had just arrived in America. She did not speak English. She did not know anyone. We gave her a warm meal and a place to sit.

That moment was what Hull-House was for.

We did not want to give people charity. We wanted to be neighbors. We wanted to learn from them too. They taught us about strength and hard work and love.

Hull-House grew. More friends came to live and work with us. We added new rooms and new programs. Hundreds of people came each week.

The neighborhood outside was loud and crowded. But inside Hull-House, people felt safe. They felt seen.

I believe that people need each other. Rich and poor. New arrivals and old citizens. We are all stronger together.

That is what I learned in the first days at Hull-House. And it is still true today.
